California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
」uly 30, 
■99■
Richard P. Sybert
Director of the Office
of Planning and Research
2229 River Plaza Drive, Suite 349
Sacramento, CA 95833
Re: Your Request for Inforrnal Assistance
our File No, I-91-2 97
Dear !lr. Sybert:
this is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibil-ities as director of the office of
ef inni,ng-a;td Resea-rch and as chairman of the Governor's Council on
Growth franagement with respect to the conflict-of-interest
provisions 5f tfre politicai 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Since your
ia.ri"" request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
but insteid seeks general guidance, we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.2 uoreover, please be a!'are that
the conmission,s jurisdiction extends only to the Political 
Reform
Act. 
(Government Code Sections 81000-91015. )
OUESTION
As Director of the office of Planning and Research and
Chairman of the Governor's Councit on Growth Management, what
restrictions does the Act impose on your ability to practice law
in your private caPacitY?
1 co,r".rr."nt code sections 81ooo-91015. AII statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
commission
regulatj.ons appear at 2 California Code of Regul-ations. Section
18600, et seq.- A11 references to regulations are to TitLe 2,
oivision o of the cal-ifornia code of Regulations.
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor $/ith the
inrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (section
83114,' Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento,CA 9580刹807 ● (916)32%660


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-297
Page 2
CONCLUS ION
The Act does not prohibit pubLic officials 
from pursuing
private occupations, such as the practice of law. However, as a
public official, 
you are prohibited from making, participating in
making or in any way attenpting to use your official 
position to
influence a qovernmental decision which will- have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic
interests .
FACTS
You are the chairnan of the Governor's Council on Grorrth
Management. The council on Growth Management considers gro!'rth and
growth rnanagement issues in the state, and advises the Governor.
iou are a1s6 director of the office of Planning and Research,3 and
as director you are a nenber of the Governor's senior staff. 
As
part of the covernor's senior staff, you may take part in the
iorrnulation of recommendations to the Governor. As discussed in
our telephone conversation of JuIy 24, 1991, the Governor's office
has designated your position as director of the Office of Research
and Pl-anning as one with decisionmaking authority subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
You have become concerned that your positions on the t$ro
public agencies wi}l prohibit or restrict your ability to practice
1aw in your private capacity. You stated you currently receive
incorne from the Perrier Group for reviewing and advising with
respect to whether the company's advertisements cornply with the
law in California and other $restern states.
You are also considering joining a Ne!, York 1aw firm ("the
firm") in an 'rof counselrt capacity. currentl-y you are considering
a fixed-fee contract in which your 1a!, office would serve as the
firn's California office. 
The firn presently has no office
outside of New York, although it has dealt with some rnatters in
california, prinarily federal litigation. 
You stated that under
the proposal you are considering you would have no ownership
inteiest in the firm, nor any interest in the profits of the firn.
ANALYSI S
The Politica1 Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
conf l ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, !,rould perforrn
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
3 You stated
you have been
our telephone
your position
ｎ
ｎ
・
■
・
■
conversation of July 24, 1991 that
since January of this year.


Our File No. I-9■
-297
Page 3
In furtherance of this goaI, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
from making, participating in
making, or otherwise usj-ng his or her official position to
influence a lJovernmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
The Act does not prohibit public officials
from pursuing private occupations. However, a public official 
is
required to disqualify hinsel,f or herself frorn governmental
decisions in which the official 
has a financial interest.{
A 'tpublic official" 
is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a nember, officer,
enployee, or consultant of a state or local governnent agency.
You are a designated ernployee of the Governor's office. 
As such,
you are subject to the conflict-of-interest 
prov5-sions of the Act
with respect to the duties at the Office of Planning and Research
and with respect to any other a<rencv vou serve on which mav
influence the decisions of the Office of Plannincr and Research,
such as the Governor's Council on Growth Management. (Furth
Advice Letter, No. I-87-079. ) 5
Section 87103 provides:
An official 
has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeabte that the decision will
have a material- financial effect, distinguishable
frorn its effect on the public generally, on the
officiat or a member of his or her innediate fanily
or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
■ ■ ■
4 tn addition, rnany public agencies naintain a list of
incornpatible private activities which the agency deems to be
inappropriate for thej,r employees. You should consult with your
agency to determine if your proposed activities are otherwise
linited by the agency's List of inconpatible activities.
5 Mor"or"., the Act requires even members of advisory bodies to
compty with the disclosure and disqualif ication provisions of the
Act under some circumstances. Thus, if the Governor's Council on
Growth Management nakes a final gTovernnental decision; may contpel
or prevent a governmental decision by reason of a veto power; or
nakes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended
period of tirne have been, regularly approved hrithout significant
a:nendment or rnodification by another public official 
or
governmental agency, the nembers wilt have obligations under the
Political Reforn Act.


Our Fi■e No. ■-9■-297
Page 4
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in whj.ch the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investrnent or interest means any investment or
interest olrned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, 
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest
or greater.
Pursuant to Section 87103 (c) , any person or busj,ness that has
made any paynents to you is a source of income. According to your
facts, you receive incone from the Perrier Group. If the palments
aggregate $250 or more in the past 12 months, the Perrier Group is
a potentially disqualifying economic interest for the purposes of
the confl ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act.
In addition, you anticipate receiving income from a New York
law firn under a fixed fee contract. 
You stated you would not
acquire an ownership interest in the firm or any interest in the
profits of the firm. 
As an enployee of the firm you will also
have an economic interest j-n the firm pursuant to Section
87103 (d), $rhether or not you receive income fron the firm.
However, absent an ownership interest in the firm of 10 percent or
greater, you hrill not have an economic interest in the clients of
the firm, 
(Section 82030. )
consequently, you may not participate in any decision that
wilI have a reasonably foreseeable and materiaL financial effect
on the Perrj.er Group or the firm. 
P1ease be aware that
participation in governmental decisions has been interpreted
broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act. Participation


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-297
Page 5
j-nctudes voting, making an appointment, committing an agency to a
course of action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf
of the agency, determining not to act, negotiating, advising or
makj,ng recommendations to the decision-maker.
In addition, rrhere a public official contacts, appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the official's 
agency concerning a
governmental decision, the official 
is considered to have used his
official position to influence the decision. (Regulations 18700
and L8700.1, copies enclosed. )
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantiat l-ikelihood that it wiII occur. certainty is not
required. Hos/ever, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops.
198.) whether the financial consequences of any decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
In addition, the foreseeable effect on your economic interest
rnust also be material to require disqual if ication. 
The Cornnission
has adopted differing guidelines to deterrnine lrhether an effect is
material, depending on the specific circumstances of each
decisi.on. For example, hrhere an econornic interest is directly
before your agency, as an applicant or the subject of the
decj,sion, Regulation La7o2.1(a) provides that the effect of the
decision on an econonic interest is deened naterial and
disqual ification is required. 
(conbs Advice Letter, No.
A-89-177 . )
Thus, if the Perrier Group or the firm initiates a Proceeding
before your agency by filing an application, clairn, appeal, or
similar request, is named as a party in a proceeding before your
alJency, or a decision of your agency involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, pernit, or
other entitlement to, or contract lrith, the Perrier Group or the
firm, you nust disqualify yourself from any participation in the
decisj.on. (Regulation La702.1(b).)
If the Perrier Group or the firn is not directly before your
agency, but may be indirectly affected, Regulation La702.2
applies.6 whether the indirect effect on a business entity, such
as the Perrier croup or the firm, is material depends on the
6 _^
' If a source of incone is an individual who may be indirectly
affected, Regulation L8702.6 provides the effect of a decision is
rnateriaL if the decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by 91r000 or morei or the decision will
affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered naterial under Section 18702.3 or section L8702.4.


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-297
Page 6
financial size of the business entity. 
For example, Regulation
La702.2 (g) provides that for a relatively sma1l business entity,
the indirect effect of a decision is material where:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or nore; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reduci-ng or elirninating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the anount of $2,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or nore.
consequently, you may not participate in any decisions that
will indirectly result in the Perrier Group or the firm incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or elininating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or
more, or will resul,t in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues or the value of assets or liabilities 
for a fiscal year
of $l-o, 000 or more.
However, even if you determine that you have an economic
interest that will be f inancj-a11y affected by a decj.sion, you may
stiI1 participate if the decision's effect on your interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
For
example, for the ttpublic generallytt exception to apply, .the
decision must affect the Perrier Group or the taw firrn in
substantialty the same nanner as it wiII affect a significant seg-
ment of the population of the state. 
(Section a7lo3; Regulation
18703.) since the exception is necessarily fact dependent and
must be appJ-ied on a decj's ion-by-decision basis, rre can only
provide this general outline.
Finally, while you just recently took office and have not
speciflcally asked about post-enptoyment restrictions on your
attivities, 
please be aware the Act provides that a EggeE state
adrninistrative official 
nay not represent another person for
cornpensation before a court or adrninistrative agency in any
jud-icia1, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in which the former
state administrative officiat participated. 
(Sections 87400 -
87405. )
The neI^I revolving door provisions were added to the Act
effective January 7, L991. These provisions provide that no
forrner designated enployee of a state adninistrative agency shall
act as agenf or attoiney for, or otherwise represent any person by


・    ｀  Our Fi■e No. I-9■-297
Page 7
formal or informal appearance or by oral, or written conmunication
before any state administrative agency subject to the direction
and controf of the governor for compensation for one year after
leaving office. (Sections a7406.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, or a
specific decision for which you need assistance, pLease feel, free
to contact ne at (916) 322-59oL.
sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
By: John w. wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
SH : JWW: aa
